coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional Purposes of Composite TiO2/CuO/Carbon Dots (CDs): Faster Photodegradation Pesticide Wastewater and High Performance Electromagnetic Wave Absorber
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Fahri, Ahmad Nurul
Ilyas, Sultan
Anugrah, Muhammad Angga
Heryanto, Heryanto
Azlan, Muhammad
Ola, Andi Tessiwoja Tenri
Rahmat, Roni
Yudasari, Nurfina
Tahir, Dahlang
coredata.dc:description: The multifunction material for EMI absorber and photocatalytic of composite TiO2/CuO/Carbon Dots (CDs) by S-scheme has been successfully fabricated composite by the solid-state method as a green photocatalyst. In this work, the CDs were synthesized from Senna Siamea seeds by solid-state method for various calcination temperatures: 250°C, 275°C, and 300°C. The CDs is used to help electron/hole charge in the photocatalysts system and for enhancing electromagnetic wave absorber. The wt.% of TiO2 for every composition keep constant is 50% and the ratio of CuO: CDs was various: 45:5, 40:10, and 30:20 for every calcination temperature of CDs. The surface morphology was sticky-tiny-balls-like shape indicating the CDs were attached and sticky surrounds TiO2 and CuO. For the size, the grain distribution increase with increasing calcination temperature which consistent with diffraction spectra, confirm that the CDs were successfully doped in composites TiO2/CuO/CDs. The composite TiO2/CuO/CDs show high electromagnetic wave absorber with efficiency 90% indicated by the reflection loss below -10 dB with working frequency from 5.54 to 6.76 GHz and the bandwidth from 1.4 to 1.8 GHz. The best photocatalytic performance is for TiO2: CuO: CDs is 50: 45: 5 with temperature calcination CDs 250°C which was degraded fipronil pesticide for the concentration of 0.05% up to 62% only for 15 minutes and shows high stability up to fourth cycles. The four-time cycles of pesticide show high stability with degradation >58% indicated that the TiO2/CuO/CDs material, effective and efficient energy for mass production pesticide wastewater. The composite in this study shows high potentials as a new bi functional material with high photocatalytic pesticide performance and absorber electromagnetic waves.
coredata.dcterms:subject: photocatalytic
pesticide
CDs
absorber electromagnetic wave
multifunction materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration mechanism of synthesizing of CDs from Senna Siamea seeds

The fluorescent CDs when irradiated by UV rays. (a) 250°C, (b) 275°C, and (c) 300°C. We have included the theoretical explanation from Ref. [46] for comparison which the blue color comes from the shell of CDs which indicated the higher bandgap.
Schematic illustration of preparation procedure for synthesizing composite TiO2/CuO/CDs
Histogram data of the sample's grain distribution by the diameter determined from Figure B1, B3, D1, and D3
XRD spectra (a) all samples, (b) comparison spectra for a different composition, (c) comparison spectra for different calcination temperatures of CDs.
FTIR spectra (a) all samples, (b) comparison spectra for a different composition, (c) comparison spectra for different calcination temperatures of CDs.
Schematic illustration of CuO and TiO2 in the carbon network for 2 and 3 dimensional (a) and electromagnetic wave absorption process by TiO2/CuO/CDs composite (b).
Reflection loss as a function of working frequencies for different thickness of the composite in this study.
UV-Visible spectra for pesticide degraded by sample A (TiO2/CuO)

UV-Visible spectra of pesticide degradation by TiO2/CuO/CDs composites. We have included the UV-Vis spectra of 0 min (non-irradiation or dark condition). We measured several (-25, -15, 10, 5,m15 minutes) times for the dark condition.
Degradation percentage and possible S-scheme photocatalytic process inside the TiO2/CuO/CDs material.
The stability of TiO2/CuO/CDs (B1) composite in degrading pesticide up to 4 cycles
Crystallite Size, dislocation density, reflection loss, work frequency, and bandwidth of composite TiO2/CuO/CDs

The reported reference for TiO2, composite TiO2-based, composite CuO-based, composite CDs-based and their degradation performance, type of pollutant, and environmental materials during preparation for comparison composite TiO2/CuO/CDs in this study

row.entry: Ratio TiO2: CuO: CDs is 50: 45: 5. Temperature CDs 250oC
10 mg/L aqueous MB solution using 20 ml H2O2 (30 wt.%) 20 mg of as prepared CuO

head.ce:title: Bifunctional Purposes of Composite TiO2/CuO/Carbon Dots (CDs): Faster Photodegradation Pesticide Wastewater and High Performance Electromagnetic Wave Absorber

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departement of Physics, Hasanuddin University, Makassar, 90245, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1Departement of Physics, Hasanuddin University, Makassar, 90245, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departement of Physics, Chungbuk National University, Cheongju, 361-763, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Centre for Physics, Indonesian Institute of Science, PUSPIPTEK, Tangerang Selatan, 15314, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Centre for Physics, Indonesian Institute of Science, PUSPIPTEK

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 3Research Centre for Physics, Indonesian Institute of Science, PUSPIPTEK, Tangerang Selatan, 15314, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The multifunction material for EMI absorber and photocatalytic of composite TiO2/CuO/Carbon Dots (CDs) by S-scheme has been successfully fabricated composite by the solid-state method as a green photocatalyst. In this work, the CDs were synthesized from Senna Siamea seeds by solid-state method for various calcination temperatures: 250°C, 275°C, and 300°C. The CDs is used to help electron/hole charge in the photocatalysts system and for enhancing electromagnetic wave absorber. The wt.% of TiO2 for every composition keep constant is 50% and the ratio of CuO: CDs was various: 45:5, 40:10, and 30:20 for every calcination temperature of CDs. The surface morphology was sticky-tiny-balls-like shape indicating the CDs were attached and sticky surrounds TiO2 and CuO. For the size, the grain distribution increase with increasing calcination temperature which consistent with diffraction spectra, confirm that the CDs were successfully doped in composites TiO2/CuO/CDs. The composite TiO2/CuO/CDs show high electromagnetic wave absorber with efficiency 90% indicated by the reflection loss below -10 dB with working frequency from 5.54 to 6.76 GHz and the bandwidth from 1.4 to 1.8 GHz. The best photocatalytic performance is for TiO2: CuO: CDs is 50: 45: 5 with temperature calcination CDs 250°C which was degraded fipronil pesticide for the concentration of 0.05% up to 62% only for 15 minutes and shows high stability up to fourth cycles. The four-time cycles of pesticide show high stability with degradation >58% indicated that the TiO2/CuO/CDs material, effective and efficient energy for mass production pesticide wastewater. The composite in this study shows high potentials as a new bi functional material with high photocatalytic pesticide performance and absorber electromagnetic waves.

ce:section.ce:para: Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division, United Nation was reported at the World Population Prospects 2019, the human population will increase from 7.7 billion people in 2019 to becomes 8.5 billion in 2030 [1]. The number of populations in the future shows that the demands of human needs for supporting daily life will increase sharply especially the electronic devices for communication and clean water. The effectiveness and efficiency of electronic devices and water treatment technology become the focus of the scientist for providing the human needs [2–11].

The effect of the increasing number of electronic devices is electromagnetic interference (EMI) which is one of the new category environmental pollutions that can be affected to human health [9–12]. For solving EMI, the technology is needed for the development of new electromagnetic wave absorbing material (EMAM), which is also can be applied for satellite, television, local area network, and defends military [3,11–13]. The main parameter of EMAM is reflection loss (RL) which indicated the ability of a material to absorb the electromagnetic wave [3,14,15]. The minimum value RL for effectiveness absorbing is reached below -10 dB. Some reference was reported for EMAM which focuses on the development of new effective material in absorbing EM with wide bandwidth, light, and low cost [11,16,17]. Materials that have been known for this purpose such as: Fe3O4 [3,16], CuO [12,18,19], CoFe2O4 [11,20,21], Co3Fe7 [11], carbon [14,22], Mn1-xZnxFe2O4 [23], and TiO2 [24–28].

The availability and easy access to clean water have become attention for scientists by recycling wastewater or treatment wastewater [5,7,8]. The activity for supporting daily life will remain side effect ex. pollution in water which is decreased the availability of clean water for consumption. These activities were containing toxic chemicals: agriculture industry using pesticides, pharmaceutical industry remain waste, and industrial textile also remains wastewater [29–32]. Pollution in water must be handled carefully because the effect on the environment and for the long term also affects the living body. It is being reported in Ref. [33] that around four billion people on the earth cannot enjoy the clean water in their lives and there are millions of them passed away every year. This situation caused by the pollutant contained in water like metals and pesticides. For pesticide is increasing in every year, where pesticide takes the main role for food production in agriculture industry [34,35]. Pesticide is the second biggest contributing pollution of wastewater which high risk to the human and animals [29,30].

The researchers in the world try to find a solution by introducing material that can be easily for implementation in the community and naturally working by capturing the sunlight which is called photocatalyst material. The photocatalyst start by absorbing the sunlight to produce radical atoms then, the radical atom will disturb chemical compounds of pollutants that are harmful to water to become harmless products [36]. The research for photocatalyst material focuses on modification material to form composite or modification of the working principle for suppressing recombination of the charged particle [37–40]. The materials that have been reported for photocatalyst applications such as TiO2 [29,39,41] and CuO [33,42,43] and working principle, some of the reference was reported for carbon as a co-catalysator [44]. Based on our knowledge, there is no references reported for composite TiO2/CuO by using the carbon dots (CDs) as co-catalysator for suppressing recombination in photocatalyst and for attenuating an electromagnetic wave as absorber materials. Hence, in this study, we synthesize ternary compound materials TiO2/CuO/CDs for photocatalyst of pesticide and absorber of electromagnetic wave. As reported by Ref. [45], the CDs were used as a co-catalysator for increasing the efficiency of photocatalysts and attenuators for electromagnetic waves as an absorber material. The CDs play essential role for modifying electronic properties of composite more environmentally friendly due to their low toxicity, aqueous stability, and enhanced surface area [46]. The CDs were synthesized from Senna Siamea seeds which are inedible. The amount of TiO2 was always 50% and for CuO and CDs was varied by the ratio 45:5, 40:10, and 30:20, respectively for every calcination temperature of CDs. The structural properties of the composites were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), surface morphology and distribution particles by SEM-EDX, and chemical bonding was determined by Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR). The absorbance of the liquid pollutant was neutralized by the composites measured by UV-Visible spectroscopy, and the electromagnetic (EM) wave absorption properties were determined by Vector Network Analyzer (VNA).

The materials in this study are CuO, TiO2, CDs, fipronil pesticide, aquadest, and PVA. CuO was purchased from Merck with particle size < 10 μm and assay 98% and TiO2 was supplied from Kimiapedia company, Surabaya Indonesia. The CDs were synthesized from Senna Siamea seeds.

Senna SIamea is a family Leguminosae which has been widely planted in many countries for erosion control, windbreaks, and ornamental tree for planting along roadsides [47,48]. The seed is a waste, the farmer usually burn in open area. To increase the economic and function of Senna Siamea seeds in this study used for producing CDs which can be applied for many purposes. The CDs were synthesized from Senna Siamea seeds by using the solid-state method. Firstly, Senna Siamea seeds are calcined in three variations of calcination temperature: 250°C, 275°C, and 300°C using Lab Tech Muffle Furnace. The black seeds were crushed then filtered using a mesh 200 to get the fine powder. The fine powder dissolved in aquadest then irradiated using ultraviolet (UV) laser to find out if the CDs were successfully synthesized from the seeds, where the CDs shall be fluorescent when it is irradiated by UV ray [49] then CDs ready to use in next step. The blue color comes from the shell of CDs which indicated the higher bandgap [50]. The schematic illustration procedure of synthesizing CDs and the fluorescence CDs during irradiation by UV rays can be seen in Figs. 1 and 2
, respectively.

The wt.% of TiO2 for every composition keep constant is 50% and the ratio of CDs: CuO was various: 45:5, 40:10, and 30:20 for every calcination temperature of CDs. The samples named are B1, B2, B3, C1, C2, C3, D1, D2, and D3, where B, C, and D is calcination temperature of CDs at 250°C, 275°C, and 300°C, respectively and number 1, 2, and 3 is composition ratio of CuO: CDs are 45:5, 40:10, and 30:20, respectively.

The TiO2 was mixed with CuO and CDs using RETSCH MM400 at a frequency of 10 Hz for 60 minutes to form a powder. The 30 mL aquadest was added to the powder and stirred for 15 minutes at room temperature. The resulting solution was then heated for 9 hours at 80°C to evaporate the water from the samples that will obtain composite TiO2/CuO/CDs. For the EM wave absorber sample, the composite powder is then mixed with PVA solution 2%, printed into pellets with thicknesses 3 mm and 4 mm, and then heated for 5 hours at 80°C using Lab Tech Muffle Furnace. The schematic procedure of TiO2/CuO/CDs preparation and the pellet samples modeled as electromagnetic wave absorber material can be seen in Figs. 3 .

The crystal structures of the composites were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD Shimadzu 7000) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The surface morphology and the distribution of the sample's grain were investigated by SEM-EDX. The chemical bonding of composites was determined by Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR). The absorbance of liquid pollutants which was neutralized by the composites was observed by UV-Visible spectroscopy and the electromagnetic (EM) wave absorption properties of the composite were determined by Vector Network Analyzer (VNA).

The photocatalytic properties of the samples were tested by the photocatalytic degradation of pesticide (Fipronil (C12H4Cl2F6N4OS)) which typical pesticide frequently used in Indonesia. For the photocatalytic test, about 0.25 g of composite TiO2/CuO/CDs material was dissolved into 100 ml of pesticides solution (the concentration of the pesticide was 0.05%) at room temperature then irradiated by halogen lamps (300 W, OSRAM 645, Germany) which is the distance of 30 cm from pesticide solution. The test was done for 15 to 45 minutes, which every 15 minutes the pesticide solution is characterized by using UV-Visible spectroscopy.

The surface morphological by SEM of the composite TiO2/CuO/CDs are shown in Fig. 4 for B1, B3, D1, and D3 were taken to represent the morphological surface of whole samples for the composition and calcination temperature of CDs. The four samples show that the surface morphology was a sticky-tiny-balls-like shape. The grain distribution by their diameter increase follows the calcination temperature of CDs and the number of CDs in the composite as shown in Fig. 4. For grain diameter increase when more CDs in composite means that more CDs were attached surrounds the TiO2/CuO composite, where less grain size indicated a greater number of the grain. The crystallite size and chemical bonding will explain from the quantitative analysis of XRD and FTIR spectra, respectively.

XRD full pattern of the composite TiO2/CuO and TiO2/CuO/CDs in this study are shown in Fig. 5 (a). Diffraction spectra of TiO2 contained in the whole composite samples were analyzed and compared with spectra from Refs. [37,50] which have characteristics of anatase phase and CuO monoclinic from Refs. [12,30]. CDs were analyzed and compared diffraction spectra were taken from Refs. [37,40] which confirms that the CDs were successfully doped in composites TiO2/CuO in this study. CuO based on JCPDS No: 89-5899 shows crystalline planes (hkl) for diffraction peak 35.4° is (002), 38.9° is (200), 58.3° is (202), 66.4° is (310), 68.1o is (220), and 75° is (004). For TiO2 based on JCPDS No: 21-1272, the crystalline plane (hkl) at diffraction peak 25.2° is (101), 48° is (200), 53.8° is (105), 55° is (211), and 62.1° is (213). There is also an additional diffraction peak that formed as a result of additional CDs at 37.7o which indicated the carbonous phase. The average of crystallite size (D) of the composites TiO2/CuO/CDs were determined from the each several pronounced peaks (25.2° (101), 35.4° (002), 38.9° (200), 48° (200), 53.8° (105), and 58.3° (202)) diffraction spectra by using the Scherrer method and dislocation density (δ) as follows [12]:
(1)
D =
0.98
λ
β
c o s θ
(2)
δ =
1
D 2
where λ is the X-ray wavelength, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak, and θ is the diffraction peaks angle. The average crystallite size and dislocation density are presented in Table 1 and show that the addition of CDs from 5% to 10% increases the crystallite size, and then decreases by an additional 20% that explained in Fig. 6 (b). Fig. 6 (b) shows the shift of peaks and the changes of β as the effect of additional CDs. As shown, the peak shifted to the higher diffraction peak 2θ position but β1 has a smaller intensity than β which contributed to an increase in the crystallite size of B1 compared with that of composite TiO2/CuO (A). It is also confirmed that the effect of calcination temperature of CDs on the crystallite size of composites as shown in Fig. 6 (c) indicated by the β2 smaller value than that of β1 and the peaks shifted to the lower diffraction peak 2θ position. This phenomenon is also affecting the diameter of composite TiO2/CuO/CDs which was confirmed by the SEM analysis in Fig. 4.

FTIR full spectra of each composite in this study are shown in Fig. 6 (a). It was confirmed that at the wavenumber 3435 cm−1 and 2364 cm−1 are assigned to O-H stretching, at 2920 cm−1 and 2856 cm−1 assigned C-H bonding, broadband at 500-700 cm−1 confirmed the fingerprint spectra of Ti-O-O [37] and Cu-O [12]. There are also some stretching bonds confirmed in the range 1120-1800 cm−1 which was zoomed as shown in Fig. 6 (b) for comparison spectra in different compositions and Fig. 6 (c) for comparison spectra in different CDs calcination temperature. At 1741 cm−1 confirmed C=O bond which increases follows the amount of the CDs in the composites TiO2/CuO/CDs and a bit increase as the increase of the CDs calcination temperature and similar for the C=C bond was confirmed at 1637 cm−1 and 1516 cm−1. The transmittance at 1637 cm−1 greatly decreases as the calcination temperature of CDs increases due to more CDs being attached to the surface of TiO2/CuO which decreases the C=C bonding. C-H bond confirmed at 1457 cm−1 shows an increase as additional of CDs which is similar for C-O bond at the wavenumber 1163 cm−1 [51]. These results were confirmed that CDs successfully bond with the Ti or Cu in composites TiO2/CuO/CDs.

The illustration of the composite in absorbing the electromagnetic wave is shown in Fig. 7 (b). The electromagnetic wave which propagates inside the material will lose its energy whether in the form of heat or vibration of the particle inside the material. The electromagnetic waves which propagate out of the material decrease ∼90% indicated by the reflection loss up to -18 dB. Reflection loss and work frequency of samples are shown in Fig. 8 and the corresponding data are shown in Table 1. It can be seen that all composite TiO2/CuO/CDs have the same trend, that have work frequency from 5.54 to 6.76 GHz for thickness 3 mm and shifted to work frequency from 5.4 to 5.95 GHz for thickness 4 mm, bandwidth from 1.4 to 1.8 GHz for every thickness, reflection loss from -18.66 to -15.53 dB for thickness 3 mm and from -18.86 to -14.37 dB for thickness 4 mm. Almost all samples have the same trend that's the working frequency shifted to the lower frequency as additional of CDs. Besides that, it is can be seen that all sample have reflection loss below -10 dB which indicate that the samples are suitable to use as electromagnetic wave absorber material.

Fig. 9 shows the UV-Visible spectrum of pesticide which degrade by sample A (TiO2/CuO) up to 30 min. As can be seen that the pesticide was not being degraded by the composite. The absorbance is increased may be due to the TiO2/CuO composite being spread in the pesticide without degrading it and making the pesticide less translucent. The CDs are then added to become a stabilizer between TiO2 and CuO and act as a co-catalysator in the photocatalytic process.

Fig. 10 shows UV-Visible spectra of pesticide degradation by TiO2/CuO/CDs composites. The degradation percentage of pesticide was calculated by using equation (3) below which the range of the taken wavelength is shown in Fig. 10 B1.
(3)
D =
∑
λ 1
λ 2
(
C
0 λ
−
C
t λ
C
0 λ
)
λ 2
−
λ 1
x
100
where C0λ is initial absorbance intensity, Ctλ is the absorbance intensity at time t, λ1 = 220 nm, λ2 = 305 nm, and λ step = 0.2 nm. The first D calculation is starts by summation λ1 = 220 nm multiplied by ((C0-Ct)/C0) increased by step 0.2 nm up to 305 nm and for the next D depend on the initial absorbance intensity C0λ and absorbance intensity at time t Ctλ.

All samples have great photocatalytic properties which can degrade fipronil up 62% just for 15 to 45 minutes as can be seen in Fig. 11 (a) to (b). The higher and the quicker degradation percentage is reached by B1 sample (CDs 250°C, ratio TiO2: CuO: CDs is 50:45:5) which degrade fipronil up to 62% just for 15 minutes. Degradation percentage shows that the addition of CDs affects the photocatalytic performance of the composite. It is indicating that CDs play an important role in modification the electron-hole pair excitation properties of the material. Besides that, SEM and XRD analysis states as the increase of calcination temperature of CDs, the diameter of the composite's grain also increases. Related to this section, it can be concluded that the size and the number of the grain also have a role in increasing photocatalytic performance. The less size of the grain and the greater number of the grain, the more H2O and O2 will be attached and the number of radicals (OH−) that are created will increase consequently increasing the photocatalytic performance. The decrease of degradation percentage may be due to some reason like the radical failed to spit up the chemical bonding of the pollutant, the occurrence of recombination of electron-hole pair, and the recombination of the pollutant chemical bonding that have been split up before. Besides that, the D sample which contains CDs with a calcination temperature of 300°C, and the sample that contain 20% of CDs has a more stable degradation percentage which explains that the calcination temperature and the addition of CDs have a main role to stabilize the photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CuO/CDs composites.

The possible faster photocatalytic process inside the material to degrade the pollutant is probably due to the presence of the S-scheme process as also shown in Fig. 11. The S-scheme within two semiconductors is effective in separating photo-generated carries and transferring them by using an internal electric field that generated and, at the same time, promotes visible light absorption, which enhances photocatalytic activity [52]. The S-scheme process is happening when the electron and hole from one material are excited and transferred to the other and form an S pattern. The carbon act as a co-catalysator which prevents the electron-hole pair recombination and as a mediator of electron and hole from the TiO2/CuO to the target pollutant [44,53–57]. The less recombination process indicated high concentration electrons and holes will produce radical O2 − and OH− which will be breaking the bond in the pesticide chemical compound [53–57]. The complete degradation mechanism of pesticide can be understood by the following chemical reaction of TiO2/CuO/CDs:
(4)
T i
O 2
/ C u O + h v → C u O / Z n O

V B

)

]
(5)
T i
O 2
/ C u O
[

)
+

)

]
→ T i
O 2
(

)
+ C u O
(
e −
)
(6)
C u O
(

)
+
O 2
→
O 2
* −
(7)
T i
O 2
(
h +
)
+
H 2
O → O
H *

H +
(8)
O
H *
+ p e s t i c i d e
(
d y e
)
→ d e g .
p r o d u c t s + C
O 2
+
H 2
O

For comparison between results in this study with the previous reported, clearly presented in Table 2 . The shorter time in degradation is 15 min for TiO2/CuO/CDs and PMS/CuO400 [58] but for pure ZnO, CuO and composite CuO/ZnO need 3 hours for 30-54%, 60-85%, 97 - 98%, respectively [59]. We can see clearly in Table 2 for CuO/TiO2 need 90 min for degrading chlorpyrifos up to 60%, which higher in this study up to 62% in very short time, only 15 min.

The stability of the TiO2/CuO/CDs material in this study for degradation of pesticide is clearly shown in Fig. 12 . The four-time cycles of pesticide show similar degradation >58% indicated that the TiO2/CuO/CDs material, effective and efficient energy in the mass production for pesticide wastewater.

Composite TiO2/CuO/CDs for electromagnetic wave absorber and S-scheme photocatalytic as a bifunction green composite materials have been successfully prepared by a simple method. In this study, the CDs were made by a simple method was used as a co-catalyst in photocatalytic and then become absorber when the electromagnetic wave incoming to the composite by vibration system. The composite TiO2/CuO/CDs for an electromagnetic wave absorber material shows the working frequency shifted to the lower frequency when the calcination temperature of CDs increases. As a photocatalytic material, composite TiO2/CuO/CDs have a great S-scheme performance where the CDs play an important role in modification the electron-hole pair excitation properties. The calcination temperature of CDs in composite also has a role in increasing photocatalytic performance. The CDs affect the grain size and the number of grain size in the composite, which less grain size indicated a greater number of the grain, the more H2O and O2 will be attached, and the number of radicals O2 − and OH− that created will increase consequently increases the photocatalytic performance. All the composite TiO2/CuO/CDs are having reflection loss below -10 dB for an electromagnetic wave absorber and have a great photocatalytic pesticide wastewater performance which indicates that the composite in this study is effective and efficient in using energy for mass production.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declare that they have no known competing financial or personal relationship that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zangiabadi, M., Saljooqi, A., Shamspur, T., & Mostafavi, A. (2019). Evaluation of GO nanosheets decorated by CuFe2O4 and CdS nanoparticles as photocatalyst for the degradation of dinoseb and imidacloprid pesticides. Ceram. Int..

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of Fe3O4/CdS–ZnS nanostructure and its application for photocatalytic degradation of chlorpyrifos pesticide and brilliant green dye from aqueous solutions
Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Soltani-nezhad, F., Saljooqi, A., Mostafavi, A., & Shamspur, T. (2019). Synthesis of Fe3O4/CdS–ZnS nanostructure and its application for photocatalytic degradation of chlorpyrifos pesticide and brilliant green dye from aqueous solutions. Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf., 109886.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of heterostructured ternary Cd/CdS/BiOCl photocatalysts for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater
Inorg. Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, J., Lu, T., Zhao, Z., Xu, R., Li, Y., Huang, Y., Tang, Y. (2020). Preparation of heterostructured ternary Cd/CdS/BiOCl photocatalysts for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater. Inorg. Chem. Commun., 108236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel visible light active rare earth doped CdS nanoparticles decorated reduced graphene oxide sheets for the degradation of cationic dye from wastewater
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Devendran, P., Selvakumar, D., Ramadoss, G., Sivaramakrishnan, R., Alagesan, T., Jayavel, R., & Pandian, K. (2022). A novel visible light active rare earth doped CdS nanoparticles decorated reduced graphene oxide sheets for the degradation of cationic dye from wastewater. Chemosphere, 287, 132091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of 17α-ethynylestradiol by crosslinked CdS nano-rod/TiO2 (B) nano-belt composite
Process. Saf. Environ. Prot.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Luo, L., Long, J., Zhao, S., Dai, J., Ma, L., Wang, H., Jiang, F. (2019). Effective visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of 17α-ethynylestradiol by crosslinked CdS nano-rod/TiO2 (B) nano-belt composite. Process. Saf. Environ. Prot., 130, 77–85.
